did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning, specifically the Xception architecture (a Convolutional Neural Network), to automatically grade the malignant degree (risk level: low-risk, high-risk, dangerous) of skin tumors using clinical images. The AI system was trained on the XiangyaDerm dataset and its performance was evaluated against 20 professional dermatologists.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the construction of the XiangyaDerm dataset (4,500 images), the development of a deep learning classifier using the Xception architecture, and the evaluation of its performance against 20 professional dermatologists. It reports specific results such as AUC scores (0.959, 0.919, 0.947) and accuracy (82.7%), which are characteristic of a primary research study rather than a review.

title: The Application of Deep Learning in the Risk Grading of Skin Tumors for Patients Using Clinical Images
brief_summary: This study developed a deep learning system to automatically grade the malignant risk of skin tumors using clinical images. The researchers constructed the XiangyaDerm dataset, comprising 4,500 pathologically verified images of ten skin tumor types collected over five years. An Xception convolutional neural network was trained to classify tumors into low-risk, high-risk, and dangerous categories. The model achieved an overall accuracy of 82.7% and area under the curve (AUC) scores of 0.959, 0.919, and 0.947 for the three risk levels. Performance evaluation against 20 professional dermatologists demonstrated that the AI classifier outperformed human experts in grading malignant degree. The system is proposed as a helpful tool for preliminary screening to alert patients to seek further medical examination.

all_task_types.target: Malignant degree risk grade (low-risk, high-risk, dangerous)
all_task_types.details: The paper describes a multi-class classification task where the Xception deep learning model predicts one of three discrete diagnostic categories for skin tumors: low-risk (benign), high-risk (low-malignant), or dangerous (high-malignant). The model was trained on the XiangyaDerm dataset and evaluated using accuracy (82.7%) and AUC scores (0.959, 0.919, 0.947) for these discrete classes.

datasets_used.name: XiangyaDerm
datasets_used.description: A private dataset collected from Xiangya Hospital Central South University over 5 years (2014-2018) using a professional digital camera (Canon, 350dpi). It contains pathologically verified clinical images of 10 specific skin tumor types selected from a larger pool of 150,223 images covering 543 skin diseases. The dataset was used to build the primary risk grading classifier.

use.training: Used to train the Xception deep learning classifier (3375 images).
use.validation: Not explicitly reported as a separate split; the remaining data after test split was used for training.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the classifier's performance (1125 images).
use.other: A subset of 60 images (20 per class) from this dataset was used for a comparative competition against 20 professional dermatologists.

datasets_used.name: Hemangioma and Syringoma Test Set
datasets_used.description: A test set consisting of images of benign dermatosis diseases not included in the training set, used to verify the system's preliminary malignant screening function on unseen disease types.

use.testing: Used to test the AI system's accuracy on diseases outside the training set.

datasets_used.name: External Racial Diversity Test Set
datasets_used.description: A test set compiled from three public datasets (Atlas Derm, DermIS, and Derm101) to evaluate the system's performance on patients of other races.

use.testing: Used to evaluate the model's generalizability across different racial populations.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of the classifier across different thresholds for the three risk grades (benign, low degree, high degree).
To quantify the discriminatory ability of the classifier for each risk grade (reported as 0.959, 0.919, and 0.947).
To select the optimum threshold for classification by maximizing the sum of sensitivity and specificity minus one.
To visualize and compare the empirical probability of predicting class j given ground truth class i for the AI system versus 20 dermatologists.
To measure the proportion of correct predictions in positive and negative samples, respectively, for each risk grade.

statistical_methods_used.method: False Positive Rate (FPR) and True Positive Rate (TPR) plotting
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare the performance of the AI system against the average performance of 20 professional dermatologists.
To evaluate the validity and superiority of the proposed classifier against 20 professional dermatologists using a subset of 60 images.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). Performance was evaluated using accuracy, AUC, sensitivity, specificity, and Youden index, and compared descriptively against dermatologists without statistical significance testing.

XiangyaDerm_Test_Set.description: Test set consisting of 1125 images (1/4 of the 4500 image dataset) used to evaluate the primary classifier.
XiangyaDerm_Test_Set.notes: The AI system outperformed 20 professional dermatologists in a comparative study using a subset of 60 images (20 per class) from this dataset.

Human_Versus_Machine_Comparison.description: Comparative evaluation using 60 chosen images from the Xiangya dataset (20 images per risk grade) against 20 professional dermatologists.

Xception.performance_comparison: Outperformed dermatologists; ROC curves showed AI system points were generally above dermatologist points.

Human_Versus_Machine_Comparison.notes: Confusion matrices indicated that low degree and high degree were easier to confuse than benign for both AI and humans. AI had better overall performance.

External_Benign_Dermatosis_Set.description: Test set of 540 images of hemangiomas and syringomas (benign dermatosis not in training set) to verify preliminary malignant screening function.
External_Benign_Dermatosis_Set.notes: Results suggest the algorithm can learn uniform depth features of specific malignant degrees, allowing grading of diseases not in the training set.

External_Racial_Diversity_Set.description: Test set of 1015 images compiled from Atlas Derm, DermIS, and Derm101 datasets to evaluate performance on other races.

Xception.performance_note: Performance obviously decreased compared to XiangyaDerm results.

External_Racial_Diversity_Set.notes: The decrease in performance is attributed to different races. The paper notes this as a shortcoming and suggests increasing clinical images of different ages and ethnicities in future studies.

architecture_details.key_technique: Depth-wise separable convolution to replace the convolution action of the original Inception v3

training_details.data_augmentation: Shuffling of data order in the import process to avoid similar pictures in the same batch

classification_or_output_head.threshold_method: Youden index (sensitivity + specificity - 1) used to select the optimum threshold

preprocessing_techniques: Data filtering to remove images of skin lesions coated by colored potion to prevent misleading color characteristics
Removal of images from special body parts (e.g., finger terminal) with insufficient lesion area or excessive background
Removal of images with skin lesions covered by hair
Removal of images with excessive exudate on the skin surface
Shuffling of data order during import to avoid similar pictures in the same batch (data augmentation/regularization technique)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Shuffling of data order in the import process to avoid similar pictures in the same batch.

conclusions: The proposed deep learning system using the Xception architecture can automatically grade the malignant degree of skin tumors into three risk levels (low-risk, high-risk, dangerous) with high accuracy (82.7%) and AUC scores of 0.959, 0.919, and 0.947 respectively.
The AI classifier outperformed 20 professional dermatologists in a comparative study using a subset of 60 images, demonstrating superior performance in grading malignant degree.

The system is effective for preliminary screening, capable of identifying patients at risk and alerting them to seek further medical examination, thereby helping to avoid delayed treatment or unnecessary worry.

The AI system demonstrated generalization capability by correctly grading diseases not included in the training set (hemangioma and syringoma) with 72% accuracy, suggesting it learns uniform depth features of specific malignant degrees.

The model's performance decreased when tested on external datasets comprising patients of different races, indicating that racial diversity is a limitation and highlighting the need for more diverse clinical images in future studies.
important_remarks: The study relies on a single-center dataset (XiangyaDerm) collected from one hospital in China over five years, which limits generalizability and introduces potential population bias.

Performance significantly decreased when tested on external datasets comprising patients of different races (Atlas Derm, DermIS, Derm101), indicating a lack of racial diversity in the training data and poor cross-racial generalizability.

The comparative evaluation against 20 professional dermatologists used a very small subset of only 60 images (20 per class), which may not be sufficient to robustly validate the claimed superiority over human experts.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was reported to support the claim that the AI system outperformed dermatologists; the comparison was descriptive.

The dataset includes only 10 specific skin tumor types selected from a larger pool of 150,223 images, meaning the model does not cover all types of skin tumors and may not be applicable to other dermatological conditions.
The system is designed for preliminary screening rather than definitive diagnosis, acknowledging that it does not replace biopsy and pathological examination.

Data filtering removed images with colored potions, hair, excessive exudate, or from special body parts (e.g., finger terminal), which may limit the system's utility in real-world clinical settings where such artifacts are common.

The study claims to be the first attempt at automatic risk grading of skin tumors using deep learning, but the clinical relevance is constrained by the need for further validation in diverse populations.
